from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """Generate a natural‑language sentence from a list of triples."""
    if not triples:
        return ""

    # Mapping from predicate to readable phrase template with chaining support
    templates = {
        "numberOfMembers": "{subj} has {obj} members",
        "season": "{subj} played in the {obj} season",
        "league": "{subj} competes in the {obj} league",
        "manager": "{subj} is managed by {obj}",
        "ground": "{subj} plays at {obj}",
        "fullName": "{subj}'s full name is {obj}",
        "chairman": "{subj} is chaired by {obj}",
        "location": "{subj} is located in {obj}",
        "owner": "{subj} is owned by {obj}",
        "nickname": "{subj} is nicknamed {obj}",
        "chairmanTitle": "{subj}'s chairman holds the title {obj}",
        "isPartOf": "{subj} is part of {obj}",
        "leader": "{subj} is led by {obj}",
        "part": "{subj} includes {obj}",
        "mayor": "{subj} is governed by mayor {obj}",
        "champions": "{subj}'s champions are {obj}",
        "country": "{subj} is in {obj}",
        "club": "{subj} played for {obj}",
        "capital": "{subj}'s capital is {obj}",
        "currency": "{subj}'s currency is {obj}",
        "demonym": "People from {subj} are called {obj}",
        "language": "{subj} speaks {obj}",
        "leaderParty": "{subj}'s leader belongs to {obj}",
        "officialLanguage": "{subj}'s official language is {obj}",
        "birthPlace": "{obj} is the birthplace of {subj}",
        "youthclub": "{subj} was in youth club {obj}",
        "operator": "{subj} is operated by {obj}",
        "tenant": "{subj} is tenant of {obj}",
        "city": "{subj} is in the city of {obj}",
        "state": "{subj} is in the state of {obj}",
    }

    # Build a map from entity to triples for chaining
    entity_to_triples = {}
    for t in triples:
        if t.subject not in entity_to_triples:
            entity_to_triples[t.subject] = []
        entity_to_triples[t.subject].append(t)
        if t.object not in entity_to_triples:
            entity_to_triples[t.object] = []
        entity_to_triples[t.object].append(t)

    # Find the main subject (entity that appears most as subject)
    subject_counts = {}
    for t in triples:
        subject_counts[t.subject] = subject_counts.get(t.subject, 0) + 1

    main_subject = max(subject_counts, key=subject_counts.get) if subject_counts else triples[0].subject

    # Build the sentence by chaining related triples
    used_triples = set(id(t) for t in triples)
    used_triples.clear()
    main_parts = []
    secondary_parts = []

    # Start with the main subject and its direct attributes
    main_triples = entity_to_triples.get(main_subject, [])
    for t in main_triples:
        if id(t) not in used_triples:
            tmpl = templates.get(t.predicate, "{subj} {pred} {obj}")
            phrase = tmpl.format(subj=t.subject, pred=t.predicate, obj=t.object)
            main_parts.append((phrase, t.object))
            used_triples.add(id(t))

    # Chain remaining triples that connect to already mentioned entities
    mentioned = {main_subject}
    remaining_triples = [t for t in triples if id(t) not in used_triples]

    # Keep chaining until no more connections can be made
    changed = True
    while changed and remaining_triples:
        changed = False
        for t in remaining_triples:
            if id(t) in used_triples:
                continue
            if t.subject in mentioned:
                tmpl = templates.get(t.predicate, "{subj} {pred} {obj}")
                phrase = tmpl.format(subj=t.subject, pred=t.predicate, obj=t.object)
                secondary_parts.append((phrase, t.object))
                used_triples.add(id(t))
                mentioned.add(t.object)
                changed = True
            elif t.object in mentioned:
                tmpl = templates.get(t.predicate, "{subj} {pred} {obj}")
                phrase = tmpl.format(subj=t.subject, pred=t.predicate, obj=t.object)
                secondary_parts.append((phrase, t.subject))
                used_triples.add(id(t))
                mentioned.add(t.subject)
                changed = True
        remaining_triples = [t for t in remaining_triples if id(t) not in used_triples]

    # Join the phrases into a single fluent sentence with relative clauses
    all_parts = [phrase for phrase, _ in main_parts + secondary_parts]
    if len(all_parts) == 0:
        sentence = ""
    elif len(all_parts) == 1:
        sentence = all_parts[0] + "."
    elif len(all_parts) == 2:
        sentence = all_parts[0] + ", and " + all_parts[1] + "."
    else:
        # Use relative clauses for better flow - create complex sentence
        sentence = all_parts[0]
        for i, part in enumerate(all_parts[1:], 1):
            if i == len(all_parts) - 1:
                sentence += ", which " + part.lower() + "."
            else:
                sentence += ", " + part.lower() + "."

    return sentence

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END

You will be given the current Python program with its BLUE score and an inspiration program and your task will be to evolve the current Python program to improve the score. It is a program that converts a list of triples into natural language text. The given triples will be associated with the topic: SportsTeam. The program must implement a function called 'predict' that accepts a list of triples and generates a coherent, contextually relevant sentence that accurately represents the information contained in the triples. Ensure that the generated text is fluent, grammatically correct, and maintains the meaning of the original data. There can be multiple triples given that make up a complex sentence. Converting all those triples into one sentence will award greater score. An example of a complex sentence:
Triples:
(Antwerp | cityServed | Antwerp International Airport)
(Belgium | country | Antwerp)
(City of Brussels | capital | Belgium)
Example sentence:
"Antwerp International Airport serves the city of Antwerp which is in Belgium, where the capital is Brussels."

The 'predict' function returns that sentence as a string. Below is the list of all possible predicates that can be given as an input. With the given predicates and example triples implement the 'predict' function so it can process all the possible predicates:
